content:
First observe that the $3\times3$ grid is a bipartite graph with 5 “black’’ squares (the 4 corners and the center) and 4 “white’’ squares (the mid‐edge cells).  Since there are exactly 5 odd numbers $\{1,3,5,7,9\}$ and 4 even numbers $\{2,4,6,8\}$, the only way to have every edge join opposite parities is to put the 5 odds on the black squares and the 4 evens on the white squares.  

Hence an Isthmian filling is exactly
   – any permutation of the 5 odds among the 5 black squares (that’s $5!$ ways), and  
   – any permutation of the 4 evens among the 4 white squares (that’s $4!$ ways).  
So in total there are 
\[
5!\;\times\;4!\;=\;120\times24\;=\;2880
\]
Isthmian fillings.

Finally we must identify fillings that differ by a rotation of the whole board.  The group of rotations is cyclic of order 4, and the only rotation that fixes any filling is the identity (no nontrivial rotation can preserve all 9 distinct numbers).  By Burnside’s Lemma (or simply by noting the action is free) the number of orbits is
\[
\frac{2880}{4}\;=\;720.
\]

Answer: \(\boxed{720}.\)

The group of rotations is cyclic of order 4, and the only rotation that fixes any filling is the identity (no nontrivial rotation can preserve all 9 distinct numbers).
